<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Feu de Matsumae]][[en:Matsumae Domain]][[fa:قلمرو ماتسومائه]][[ja:松前藩]][[ko:마쓰마에번]][[ru:Мацумаэ (княжество)]][[wuu:松前藩]][[zh:松前藩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18669712]] Wikidata name: Matsumae Domain [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{refimprove|date=March 2024}} The '''Matsumae Domain''' (松前藩), a prominent domain during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], was situated in Matsumae, [[Matsumae Island]] (Ishijima), which is currently known as Matsumae Town, [[:en:Matsumae District, Hokkaido|Matsumae District]], Hokkaido, via [[:en:Tsugaru District, Mutsu|Tsugaru District]], [[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]]. The clan's leader, also known as the lord of the domain, constructed [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae]] Fukuyama Castle in the same location and henceforth, the clan came to be referred to as the Fukuyama clan. In 1868, the castle was relocated to Tatejo in Assabu-cho, Hiyama-gun, within the territory, and was known as Tatehan during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. Initially, the Matsumae clan's jurisdiction was limited to the Wajin territory on the [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] in southwest Hokkaido. However, they gradually expanded their control over the Ezo region, which is now part of Hokkaido, and transformed it into their domain. The clan's economic foundation relied on their exclusive trade with the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]] of Ezo, which set them apart from the agricultural-based governance principles of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Shogunate]] system. Towards the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shogunate often assumed control over Ezo.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Howell |first=David L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3bjADwAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PA33 |title=Capitalism From Within: Economy, Society, and the State in a Japanese Fishery |date=2018-05-04 |publisher=Univ of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-30158-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Siddle |first=Richard M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W5DKfJsPv2sC&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT47 |title=Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan |date=2012-06-14 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-82680-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Bailey |first=Scott C. M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9VzbEAAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT92 |title=Russia and Japan in the Sea of Okhotsk: A Global History of Maritime Travel and Cultural Encounters, 1600-1900 |date=2023-12-01 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-003-81876-2 |language=en}}</ref> {{short description|Japanese feudal domain located in Hokkaidō Province}} {{Infobox former subdivision |_noautocat = |native_name = 松前藩 |conventional_long_name = Matsumae Domain |common_name = Matsumae Domain |subdivision = [[:en:Han system|Han]] |nation = |status_text = [[:en:Han system|Domain]] of Japan |government_type = Daimyō
|capital = [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae Castle]] |coordinates = |political_subdiv = |today = [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]
|year_start = 1590 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post =
|image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = File:Maru ni Takedabishi (No background and Black color drawing).svg |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] |symbol = |image_map = MatsumaeJo.jpg |image_map_caption = Restored castle tower of Matsumae Castle
|stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Matsumae Norihiro.jpg|right|thumb|270px|[[Matsumae Norihiro,]] the 13th next to last daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] final daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] ==History== As per the historical account in the Matsumae domain's "[[Silla Records]]," [[:en:Takeda Nobuhiro|Takeda Nobuhiro]], believed to be a descendant of the Kai-Genji and Wakasa-[[Takeda clan]]s, was the founder during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Nobuhiro succeeded Kakizaki Suishige, appointed by Masasue Ando as the shugo of Kamigoku, assumed the Kakizaki clan name, and settled in the southern part of the present-day Oshima Peninsula. Under Kakizaki Toshihiro's leadership, with the lord Ando Shunsuke, peace was established with the Ainu of Chikomotain and Hashitain, strengthening control over Ezo. Towards the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], Tamenobu Oura led an army in the Tsugaru region, resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event hastened the Kakizaki clan's independence in the Ezo region. During [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]]'s era, the son of Tokihiro, official separation from the [[:en:Andō clan|Ando clan]] (later the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) was approved by submitting to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, submission to [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] led to the recognition of the right to rule over Ezo. In the early Edo period, the lord of [[Ezo Island]] was treated as a guest vassal, but during the fifth shogun Tsunayoshi Tokugawa's time, joining the Tokuyoriai resulted in being treated as a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]. Additionally, from 1719, becoming a feudal lord with a Rank of 10,000 koku marked a significant development in the history of the Matsumae domain's governance over Ezo. At the conclusion of the Sengoku period, [[:en:Oura Tamenobu|Oura Tamenobu]] assembled a military force in the [[Tsugaru region]], resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event played a significant role in expediting the independence of the [[:en:Kakizaki clan|Kakizaki clan]] in the Ezo region. Under the leadership of [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]], the son of Tokihiro, he gained official recognition for his separation from the Ando clan (later known as the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) by pledging allegiance to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, he further solidified his position by submitting to Tokugawa Ieyasu, thereby securing the authority to govern over Ezo. Initially, during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], he was regarded as a guest vassal and held the position of the lord of Ezo Island. However, during the reign of the fifth shogun, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], he joined the Tokuyoriai and was elevated to the status of a hatamoto. Moreover, starting from 1719, he became a feudal lord with a substantial landholding of 10,000 koku.During that period, cultivating rice in Ezo was deemed unattainable, leading to the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] holding a feudal lord position without a high Rank. The designation of 10,000 koku as a Rank came later on. The black seal bestowed upon [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] by [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Ieyasu]] in 1604 granted the clan exclusive trading rights with the [[:en:Emishi people|Emishi]] ([[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]]. Trade commodities like salmon, kelp, herring, and furs brought in revenue, reportedly amounting to 70,000 koku. The feudal lord himself dispatched trading vessels to Ezo, while his retainers were entrusted with the responsibility of establishing trading posts (akinaiba) in Ezo and authorizing them to send ships for trade. The Matsumae clan implemented a policy of regulating trade between Ezo and Wajin, designating the southern [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] as [[Wajinchi]] and the remaining area as Ezochi. Ainu individuals used to journey to Wajin territories and Honshu for trade until the early Edo period, after which control became more stringent. In 1679, the Matsumae clan established an anajinya in Kusharukotan (Kusukei, Odomari-cho, Odomari-gun) in Sakhalin, initiating the development of the region as a fishing hub. Fishing was the primary industry in Wajinchi, directly managed by the Matsumae clan, but due to dwindling herring catches, individuals began migrating to Ezo for employment opportunities. By 1834, the castle town of Matsumae had evolved into a thriving urban center with a populace exceeding 10,000 residents. [[Matsumae Nobuhiro]], the 14th lord of the domain, submitted a request for the reinstatement of the domain name on June 24, 1869, and his request was approved. Subsequently, he was designated as the governor of the Matsumae domain. During that year, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] consisted of 86 districts across 11 provinces. The Matsumae Domain was established for a period of two years before it was transformed into a prefecture on July 14, 1871. Its name derived from the fact that the [[:en:Japanese imperial court|Imperial Court]] granted permission to construct a new castle in the village of Atsubu in the western region of the country. Initially, the government office was situated at Fukuyama Castle, as the construction of the new castle was interrupted by the Hakodate War. During the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] and the [[Tohoku War]], the Matsumae clan joined forces with the Ou-etsu clan alliance, but the loyalist Seigi-tai took control of the domain and defected to the new government. They relocated to the newly built [[Tate Castle]] and fought against the former shogunate army in the Hakodate War under the name of the Tate clan. Following the war, individuals who collaborated with the former shogunate army were apprehended and punished accordingly. Over 90 individuals, including both townspeople and samurai, faced punishment, with 19 of them being publicly executed. ==Holdings at the end of the Edo period== **[[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]] *[[Erzhi County]] *[[Hiyama-gun]] *[[Tsugaru-gun]] *[[Fukushima-gun]] **[[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] *[[Ryuhi Cape]] *[[:en:Iwashiro Province|Iwashiro Province]] *[[Date County]] - 10 villages *[[Murayama County]] - 39 villages == List of daimyo == :{| class=wikitable ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' |- |colspan=6| [[File:Maru ni Takedabishi (No background and Black color drawing).svg |25px]] '''[[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]],''' 1590 - 1871 (''[[:en:Fudai daimyo|Fudai daimyo]]'') |- ||1||[[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] (松前慶広)||1590 - 1616|| ''Shima no kami, Izu no kami'' (志摩守, 伊豆守)|| Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 10,000 ''koku''|| |- ||2||[[:en:Matsumae Kinhiro|Matsumae Kinhiro]] (松前公広)||1616 - 1641|| ''Shima no kami'' (志摩守)||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 10,000 ''koku''|| |- ||3||[[Matsumae Ujihiro]] (松前氏広)||1641 - 1648|| ''None'' (なし)|| Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 10,000 ''koku''|| |- ||4||[[Matsumae Takehiro]] (松前高広)||1648 - 1665|| ''None'' (なし)|| Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 10,000 ''koku''|| |- ||5||[[Matsumae Norihiro]] (松前矩広)||1665 - 1720|| ''Shima no kami'' (志摩守)||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 10,000 ''koku''|| |- ||6||[[Matsumae Kunihiro]] (松前邦広)||1720 - 1743|| ''Shima no kami'' (志摩守)||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 10,000 ''koku''|| |- ||7||[[Matsumae Sukehiro]] (松前資広)||1743 - 1765|| ''Wakasa no kami'' (若さ の 髪)||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 10,000 --> 30,000 ''koku''|| |- ||8||[[Matsumae Michihiro]] (松前道広)||1765 - 1792|| ''Mimasaka no kami , Shima no kami'' (美作の髪 、島の髪)||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- ||9||[[:en:Matsumae Akihiro|Matsumae Akihiro]] (松前章広)||1792 - 1833|| ''Wakasa no kami'' (若さ の 髪)||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- ||10||[[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] (松前良広)||1833 - 1839|| ''None'' (なし)|| Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- ||11||[[Matsumae Masahiro]] (松前昌広)||1839 - 1849|| ''Shima no kami'' (志摩守)||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- ||12||[[:en:Matsumae Takahiro|Matsumae Takahiro]] (松前崇広)||1849 - 1865|| ''Izu no kami'' (伊豆の髪 )||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- ||13||[[Matsumae Norihiro]] (松前徳広)||1865 - 1868|| ''Shima no kami'' (志摩守)||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- ||14||{{ill|Matsumae Nagahiro|ja|松前徳広|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumae Nagahiro|1=Matsumae Norihiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8514292}} (松前徳広)||1869 - 1871|| ''None'' (なし)|| Senior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- |} ==See also== *[[:en:List of han|List of han]] *[[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]] ==References== {{Reflist}}

The clan's leader, also known as the lord of the domain, constructed [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae]] Fukuyama Castle in the same location and henceforth, the clan came to be referred to as the Fukuyama clan. In 1868, the castle was relocated to Tatejo in Assabu-cho, Hiyama-gun, within the territory, and was known as Tatehan during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. Initially, the Matsumae clan's jurisdiction was limited to the Wajin territory on the [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] in southwest Hokkaido. However, they gradually expanded their control over the Ezo region, which is now part of Hokkaido, and transformed it into their domain. The clan's economic foundation relied on their exclusive trade with the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]] of Ezo, which set them apart from the agricultural-based governance principles of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Shogunate]] system. Towards the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shogunate often assumed control over Ezo.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Howell |first=David L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3bjADwAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PA33 |title=Capitalism From Within: Economy, Society, and the State in a Japanese Fishery |date=2018-05-04 |publisher=Univ of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-30158-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Siddle |first=Richard M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W5DKfJsPv2sC&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT47 |title=Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan |date=2012-06-14 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-82680-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Bailey |first=Scott C. M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9VzbEAAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT92 |title=Russia and Japan in the Sea of Okhotsk: A Global History of Maritime Travel and Cultural Encounters, 1600-1900 |date=2023-12-01 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-003-81876-2 |language=en}}</ref> {{short description|Japanese feudal domain located in Hokkaidō Province}} {{Infobox former subdivision |_noautocat = |native_name = 松前藩 |conventional_long_name = Matsumae Domain |common_name = Matsumae Domain |subdivision = [[:en:Han system|Han]] |nation = |status_text = [[:en:Han system|Domain]] of Japan |government_type = Daimyō

|stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Matsumae Norihiro.jpg|right|thumb|270px|[[Matsumae Norihiro,]] the 13th next to last daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] final daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] ==History== As per the historical account in the Matsumae domain's "[[Silla Records]]," [[:en:Takeda Nobuhiro|Takeda Nobuhiro]], believed to be a descendant of the Kai-Genji and Wakasa-[[Takeda clan]]s, was the founder during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Under Kakizaki Toshihiro's leadership, with the lord Ando Shunsuke, peace was established with the Ainu of Chikomotain and Hashitain, strengthening control over Ezo. Towards the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], Tamenobu Oura led an army in the Tsugaru region, resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event hastened the Kakizaki clan's independence in the Ezo region. In the early Edo period, the lord of [[Ezo Island]] was treated as a guest vassal, but during the fifth shogun Tsunayoshi Tokugawa's time, joining the Tokuyoriai resulted in being treated as a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]. Additionally, from 1719, becoming a feudal lord with a Rank of 10,000 koku marked a significant development in the history of the Matsumae domain's governance over Ezo. This event played a significant role in expediting the independence of the [[:en:Kakizaki clan|Kakizaki clan]] in the Ezo region. Under the leadership of [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]], the son of Tokihiro, he gained official recognition for his separation from the Ando clan (later known as the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) by pledging allegiance to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. Initially, during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], he was regarded as a guest vassal and held the position of the lord of Ezo Island. However, during the reign of the fifth shogun, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], he joined the Tokuyoriai and was elevated to the status of a hatamoto. Moreover, starting from 1719, he became a feudal lord with a substantial landholding of 10,000 koku.During that period, cultivating rice in Ezo was deemed unattainable, leading to the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] holding a feudal lord position without a high Rank. The designation of 10,000 koku as a Rank came later on. The feudal lord himself dispatched trading vessels to Ezo, while his retainers were entrusted with the responsibility of establishing trading posts (akinaiba) in Ezo and authorizing them to send ships for trade. The Matsumae clan implemented a policy of regulating trade between Ezo and Wajin, designating the southern [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] as [[Wajinchi]] and the remaining area as Ezochi. Ainu individuals used to journey to Wajin territories and Honshu for trade until the early Edo period, after which control became more stringent. By 1834, the castle town of Matsumae had evolved into a thriving urban center with a populace exceeding 10,000 residents. [[Matsumae Nobuhiro]], the 14th lord of the domain, submitted a request for the reinstatement of the domain name on June 24, 1869, and his request was approved. Subsequently, he was designated as the governor of the Matsumae domain. During that year, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] consisted of 86 districts across 11 provinces. The Matsumae Domain was established for a period of two years before it was transformed into a prefecture on July 14, 1871. Its name derived from the fact that the [[:en:Japanese imperial court|Imperial Court]] granted permission to construct a new castle in the village of Atsubu in the western region of the country. Initially, the government office was situated at Fukuyama Castle, as the construction of the new castle was interrupted by the Hakodate War. During the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] and the [[Tohoku War]], the Matsumae clan joined forces with the Ou-etsu clan alliance, but the loyalist Seigi-tai took control of the domain and defected to the new government. They relocated to the newly built [[Tate Castle]] and fought against the former shogunate army in the Hakodate War under the name of the Tate clan. Following the war, individuals who collaborated with the former shogunate army were apprehended and punished accordingly. Over 90 individuals, including both townspeople and samurai, faced punishment, with 19 of them being publicly executed. ==Holdings at the end of the Edo period== **[[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]] *[[Erzhi County]] *[[Hiyama-gun]] *[[Tsugaru-gun]] *[[Fukushima-gun]] **[[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] *[[Ryuhi Cape]] *[[:en:Iwashiro Province|Iwashiro Province]] *[[Date County]] - revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Feu de Matsumae]][[en:Matsumae Domain]][[fa:قلمرو ماتسومائه]][[ja:松前藩]][[ko:마쓰마에번]][[ru:Мацумаэ (княжество)]][[wuu:松前藩]][[zh:松前藩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18669712]] Wikidata name: Matsumae Domain [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{refimprove|date=March 2024}} The '''Matsumae Domain''' (松前藩), a prominent domain during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], was situated in Matsumae, [[Matsumae Island]] (Ishijima), which is currently known as Matsumae Town, [[:en:Matsumae District, Hokkaido|Matsumae District]], Hokkaido, via [[:en:Tsugaru District, Mutsu|Tsugaru District]], [[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]]. The clan's leader, also known as the lord of the domain, constructed [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae]] Fukuyama Castle in the same location and henceforth, the clan came to be referred to as the Fukuyama clan. In 1868, the castle was relocated to Tatejo in Assabu-cho, Hiyama-gun, within the territory, and was known as Tatehan during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. Initially, the Matsumae clan's jurisdiction was limited to the Wajin territory on the [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] in southwest Hokkaido. However, they gradually expanded their control over the Ezo region, which is now part of Hokkaido, and transformed it into their domain. The clan's economic foundation relied on their exclusive trade with the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]] of Ezo, which set them apart from the agricultural-based governance principles of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Shogunate]] system. Towards the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shogunate often assumed control over Ezo.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Howell |first=David L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3bjADwAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PA33 |title=Capitalism From Within: Economy, Society, and the State in a Japanese Fishery |date=2018-05-04 |publisher=Univ of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-30158-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Siddle |first=Richard M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W5DKfJsPv2sC&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT47 |title=Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan |date=2012-06-14 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-82680-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Bailey |first=Scott C. M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9VzbEAAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT92 |title=Russia and Japan in the Sea of Okhotsk: A Global History of Maritime Travel and Cultural Encounters, 1600-1900 |date=2023-12-01 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-003-81876-2 |language=en}}</ref> {{short description|Japanese feudal domain located in Hokkaidō Province}} {{Infobox former subdivision |_noautocat = |native_name = 松前藩 |conventional_long_name = Matsumae Domain |common_name = Matsumae Domain |subdivision = [[:en:Han system|Han]] |nation = |status_text = [[:en:Han system|Domain]] of Japan |government_type = Daimyō
|capital = [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae Castle]] |coordinates = |political_subdiv = |today = [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]
|year_start = 1590 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post =
|image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = File:Maru ni Takedabishi (No background and Black color drawing).svg |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] |symbol = |image_map = MatsumaeJo.jpg |image_map_caption = Restored castle tower of Matsumae Castle
|stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Matsumae Norihiro.jpg|right|thumb|270px|[[Matsumae Norihiro,]] the 13th next to last daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] final daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] ==History== As per the historical account in the Matsumae domain's "[[Silla Records]]," [[:en:Takeda Nobuhiro|Takeda Nobuhiro]], believed to be a descendant of the Kai-Genji and Wakasa-[[Takeda clan]]s, was the founder during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Nobuhiro succeeded Kakizaki Suishige, appointed by Masasue Ando as the shugo of Kamigoku, assumed the Kakizaki clan name, and settled in the southern part of the present-day Oshima Peninsula. Under Kakizaki Toshihiro's leadership, with the lord Ando Shunsuke, peace was established with the Ainu of Chikomotain and Hashitain, strengthening control over Ezo. Towards the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], Tamenobu Oura led an army in the Tsugaru region, resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event hastened the Kakizaki clan's independence in the Ezo region. During [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]]'s era, the son of Tokihiro, official separation from the [[:en:Andō clan|Ando clan]] (later the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) was approved by submitting to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, submission to [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] led to the recognition of the right to rule over Ezo. In the early Edo period, the lord of [[Ezo Island]] was treated as a guest vassal, but during the fifth shogun Tsunayoshi Tokugawa's time, joining the Tokuyoriai resulted in being treated as a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]. Additionally, from 1719, becoming a feudal lord with a Rank of 10,000 koku marked a significant development in the history of the Matsumae domain's governance over Ezo. At the conclusion of the Sengoku period, [[:en:Oura Tamenobu|Oura Tamenobu]] assembled a military force in the [[Tsugaru region]], resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event played a significant role in expediting the independence of the [[:en:Kakizaki clan|Kakizaki clan]] in the Ezo region. Under the leadership of [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]], the son of Tokihiro, he gained official recognition for his separation from the Ando clan (later known as the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) by pledging allegiance to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, he further solidified his position by submitting to Tokugawa Ieyasu, thereby securing the authority to govern over Ezo. Initially, during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], he was regarded as a guest vassal and held the position of the lord of Ezo Island. However, during the reign of the fifth shogun, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], he joined the Tokuyoriai and was elevated to the status of a hatamoto. Moreover, starting from 1719, he became a feudal lord with a substantial landholding of 10,000 koku.During that period, cultivating rice in Ezo was deemed unattainable, leading to the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] holding a feudal lord position without a high Rank. The designation of 10,000 koku as a Rank came later on. The black seal bestowed upon [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] by [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Ieyasu]] in 1604 granted the clan exclusive trading rights with the [[:en:Emishi people|Emishi]] ([[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]]. Trade commodities like salmon, kelp, herring, and furs brought in revenue, reportedly amounting to 70,000 koku. The feudal lord himself dispatched trading vessels to Ezo, while his retainers were entrusted with the responsibility of establishing trading posts (akinaiba) in Ezo and authorizing them to send ships for trade. The Matsumae clan implemented a policy of regulating trade between Ezo and Wajin, designating the southern [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] as [[Wajinchi]] and the remaining area as Ezochi. Ainu individuals used to journey to Wajin territories and Honshu for trade until the early Edo period, after which control became more stringent. In 1679, the Matsumae clan established an anajinya in Kusharukotan (Kusukei, Odomari-cho, Odomari-gun) in Sakhalin, initiating the development of the region as a fishing hub. Fishing was the primary industry in Wajinchi, directly managed by the Matsumae clan, but due to dwindling herring catches, individuals began migrating to Ezo for employment opportunities. By 1834, the castle town of Matsumae had evolved into a thriving urban center with a populace exceeding 10,000 residents. [[Matsumae Nobuhiro]], the 14th lord of the domain, submitted a request for the reinstatement of the domain name on June 24, 1869, and his request was approved. Subsequently, he was designated as the governor of the Matsumae domain. During that year, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] consisted of 86 districts across 11 provinces. The Matsumae Domain was established for a period of two years before it was transformed into a prefecture on July 14, 1871. Its name derived from the fact that the [[:en:Japanese imperial court|Imperial Court]] granted permission to construct a new castle in the village of Atsubu in the western region of the country. Initially, the government office was situated at Fukuyama Castle, as the construction of the new castle was interrupted by the Hakodate War. During the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] and the [[Tohoku War]], the Matsumae clan joined forces with the Ou-etsu clan alliance, but the loyalist Seigi-tai took control of the domain and defected to the new government. They relocated to the newly built [[Tate Castle]] and fought against the former shogunate army in the Hakodate War under the name of the Tate clan. Following the war, individuals who collaborated with the former shogunate army were apprehended and punished accordingly. Over 90 individuals, including both townspeople and samurai, faced punishment, with 19 of them being publicly executed. ==Holdings at the end of the Edo period== **[[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]] *[[Erzhi County]] *[[Hiyama-gun]] *[[Tsugaru-gun]] *[[Fukushima-gun]] **[[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] *[[Ryuhi Cape]] *[[:en:Iwashiro Province|Iwashiro Province]] *[[Date County]] - 10 villages *[[Murayama County]] - 39 villages == List of daimyo == :{| class=wikitable ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' |- |colspan=6| [[File:Maru ni Takedabishi (No background and Black color drawing).svg |25px]] '''[[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]],''' 1590 - 1871 (''[[:en:Fudai daimyo|Fudai daimyo]]'') |- ||1||[[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] (松前慶広)||1590 - 1616|| ''Shima no kami, Izu no kami'' (志摩守, 伊豆守)|| Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 10,000 ''koku''|| |- ||2||[[:en:Matsumae Kinhiro|Matsumae Kinhiro]] (松前公広)||1616 - 1839|| ''None'' (なし)|| Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- ||11||[[Matsumae Masahiro]] (松前昌広)||1839 - 1849|| ''Shima no kami'' (志摩守)||Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- ||12||[[:en:Matsumae Takahiro|Matsumae Takahiro]] (松前崇広)||1849 - <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Feu de Matsumae]][[en:Matsumae Domain]][[fa:قلمرو ماتسومائه]][[ja:松前藩]][[ko:마쓰마에번]][[ru:Мацумаэ (княжество)]][[wuu:松前藩]][[zh:松前藩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18669712]] Wikidata name: Matsumae Domain [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{refimprove|date=March 2024}} The '''Matsumae Domain''' (松前藩), a prominent domain during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], was situated in Matsumae, [[Matsumae Island]] (Ishijima), which is currently known as Matsumae Town, [[:en:Matsumae District, Hokkaido|Matsumae District]], Hokkaido, via [[:en:Tsugaru District, Mutsu|Tsugaru District]], [[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]]. The clan's leader, also known as the lord of the domain, constructed [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae]] Fukuyama Castle in the same location and henceforth, the clan came to be referred to as the Fukuyama clan. In 1868, the castle was relocated to Tatejo in Assabu-cho, Hiyama-gun, within the territory, and was known as Tatehan during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. Initially, the Matsumae clan's jurisdiction was limited to the Wajin territory on the [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] in southwest Hokkaido. However, they gradually expanded their control over the Ezo region, which is now part of Hokkaido, and transformed it into their domain. The clan's economic foundation relied on their exclusive trade with the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]] of Ezo, which set them apart from the agricultural-based governance principles of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Shogunate]] system. Towards the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shogunate often assumed control over Ezo.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Howell |first=David L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3bjADwAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PA33 |title=Capitalism From Within: Economy, Society, and the State in a Japanese Fishery |date=2018-05-04 |publisher=Univ of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-30158-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Siddle |first=Richard M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W5DKfJsPv2sC&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT47 |title=Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan |date=2012-06-14 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-82680-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Bailey |first=Scott C. M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9VzbEAAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT92 |title=Russia and Japan in the Sea of Okhotsk: A Global History of Maritime Travel and Cultural Encounters, 1600-1900 |date=2023-12-01 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-003-81876-2 |language=en}}</ref> {{short description|Japanese feudal domain located in Hokkaidō Province}} {{Infobox former subdivision |_noautocat = |native_name = 松前藩 |conventional_long_name = Matsumae Domain |common_name = Matsumae Domain |subdivision = [[:en:Han system|Han]] |nation = |status_text = [[:en:Han system|Domain]] of Japan |government_type = Daimyō

|year_start = 1590 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post =
|image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = File:Maru ni Takedabishi (No background and Black color drawing).svg |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] |symbol = |image_map = MatsumaeJo.jpg |image_map_caption = Restored castle tower of Matsumae Castle
|stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Matsumae Norihiro.jpg|right|thumb|270px|[[Matsumae Norihiro,]] the 13th next to last daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] final daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] ==History== As per the historical account in the Matsumae domain's "[[Silla Records]]," [[:en:Takeda Nobuhiro|Takeda Nobuhiro]], believed to be a descendant of the Kai-Genji and Wakasa-[[Takeda clan]]s, was the founder during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Nobuhiro succeeded Kakizaki Suishige, appointed by Masasue Ando as the shugo of Kamigoku, assumed the Kakizaki clan name, and settled in the southern part of the present-day Oshima Peninsula. Under Kakizaki Toshihiro's leadership, with the lord Ando Shunsuke, peace was established with the Ainu of Chikomotain and Hashitain, strengthening control over Ezo. Towards the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], Tamenobu Oura led an army in the Tsugaru region, resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event hastened the Kakizaki clan's independence in the Ezo region. During [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]]'s era, the son of Tokihiro, official separation from the [[:en:Andō clan|Ando clan]] (later the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) was approved by submitting to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, submission to [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] led to the recognition of the right to rule over Ezo. In the early Edo period, the lord of [[Ezo Island]] was treated as a guest vassal, but during the fifth shogun Tsunayoshi Tokugawa's time, joining the Tokuyoriai resulted in being treated as a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]. Additionally, from 1719, becoming a feudal lord with a Rank of 10,000 koku marked a significant development in the history of the Matsumae domain's governance over Ezo. At the conclusion of the Sengoku period, [[:en:Oura Tamenobu|Oura Tamenobu]] assembled a military force in the [[Tsugaru region]], resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event played a significant role in expediting the independence of the [[:en:Kakizaki clan|Kakizaki clan]] in the Ezo region. Under the leadership of [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]], the son of Tokihiro, he gained official recognition for his separation from the Ando clan (later known as the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) by pledging allegiance to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, he further solidified his position by submitting to Tokugawa Ieyasu, thereby securing the authority to govern over Ezo. Initially, during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], he was regarded as a guest vassal and held the position of the lord of Ezo Island. However, during the reign of the fifth shogun, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], he joined the Tokuyoriai and was elevated to the status of a hatamoto. Moreover, starting from 1719, he became a feudal lord with a substantial landholding of 10,000 koku.During that period, cultivating rice in Ezo was deemed unattainable, leading to the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] holding a feudal lord position without a high Rank. The designation of 10,000 koku as a Rank came later on. The black seal bestowed upon [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] by [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Ieyasu]] in 1604 granted the clan exclusive trading rights with the [[:en:Emishi people|Emishi]] ([[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]]. Trade commodities like salmon, kelp, herring, and furs brought in revenue, reportedly amounting to 70,000 koku. The feudal lord himself dispatched trading vessels to Ezo, while his retainers were entrusted with the responsibility of establishing trading posts (akinaiba) in Ezo and authorizing them to send ships for trade. The Matsumae clan implemented a policy of regulating trade between Ezo and Wajin, designating the southern [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] as [[Wajinchi]] and the remaining area as Ezochi. Ainu individuals used to journey to Wajin territories and Honshu for trade until the early Edo period, after which control became more stringent. In 1679, the Matsumae clan established an anajinya in Kusharukotan (Kusukei, Odomari-cho, Odomari-gun) in Sakhalin, initiating the development of the region as a fishing hub. Fishing was the primary industry in Wajinchi, directly managed by the Matsumae clan, but due to dwindling herring catches, individuals began migrating to Ezo for employment opportunities. By 1834, the castle town of Matsumae had evolved into a thriving urban center with a populace exceeding 10,000 residents. [[Matsumae Nobuhiro]], the 14th lord of the domain, submitted a request for the reinstatement of the domain name on June 24, 1869, and his request was approved. Subsequently, he was designated as the governor of the Matsumae domain. During that year, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] consisted of 86 districts across 11 provinces. The Matsumae Domain was established for a period of two years before it was transformed into a prefecture on July 14, 1871. Its name derived from the fact that the [[:en:Japanese imperial court|Imperial Court]] granted permission to construct a new castle in the village of Atsubu in the western region of the country. Initially, the government office was situated at Fukuyama Castle, as the construction of the new castle was interrupted by the Hakodate War. During the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] and the [[Tohoku War]], the Matsumae clan joined forces with the Ou-etsu clan alliance, but the loyalist Seigi-tai took control of the domain and defected to the new government. They relocated to the newly built [[Tate Castle]] and fought against the former shogunate army in the Hakodate War under the name of the Tate clan. Following the war, individuals who collaborated with the former shogunate army were apprehended and punished accordingly. Over 90 individuals, including both townspeople and samurai, faced punishment, with 19 of them being publicly executed. ==Holdings at the end of the Edo period== **[[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]] *[[Erzhi County]] *[[Hiyama-gun]] *[[Tsugaru-gun]] *[[Fukushima-gun]] **[[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] *[[Ryuhi Cape]] *[[:en:Iwashiro Province|Iwashiro Province]] *[[Date County]] - 10 villages *[[Murayama County]] - 39 villages == List of daimyo == :{| class=wikitable ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' |- |colspan=6| [[File:Maru ni Takedabishi (No background and Black color drawing).svg |25px]] '''[[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]],''' 1590 - 1871|| ''None'' (なし)|| Senior 4th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下)|| 30,000 ''koku''|| |- |} ==See also== *[[:en:List of han|List of han]] *[[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]] ==References== {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Feu de Matsumae]][[en:Matsumae Domain]][[fa:قلمرو ماتسومائه]][[ja:松前藩]][[ko:마쓰마에번]][[ru:Мацумаэ (княжество)]][[wuu:松前藩]][[zh:松前藩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18669712]] Wikidata name: Matsumae Domain [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{refimprove|date=March 2024}} The '''Matsumae Domain''' (松前藩), a prominent domain during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], was situated in Matsumae, [[Matsumae Island]] (Ishijima), which is currently known as Matsumae Town, [[:en:Matsumae District, Hokkaido|Matsumae District]], Hokkaido, via [[:en:Tsugaru District, Mutsu|Tsugaru District]], [[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]]. The clan's leader, also known as the lord of the domain, constructed [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae]] Fukuyama Castle in the same location and henceforth, the clan came to be referred to as the Fukuyama clan. In 1868, the castle was relocated to Tatejo in Assabu-cho, Hiyama-gun, within the territory, and was known as Tatehan during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. Initially, the Matsumae clan's jurisdiction was limited to the Wajin territory on the [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] in southwest Hokkaido. However, they gradually expanded their control over the Ezo region, which is now part of Hokkaido, and transformed it into their domain. The clan's economic foundation relied on their exclusive trade with the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]] of Ezo, which set them apart from the agricultural-based governance principles of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Shogunate]] system. Towards the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shogunate often assumed control over Ezo.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Howell |first=David L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3bjADwAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PA33 |title=Capitalism From Within: Economy, Society, and the State in a Japanese Fishery |date=2018-05-04 |publisher=Univ of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-30158-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Siddle |first=Richard M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W5DKfJsPv2sC&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT47 |title=Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan |date=2012-06-14 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-82680-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Bailey |first=Scott C. M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9VzbEAAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT92 |title=Russia and Japan in the Sea of Okhotsk: A Global History of Maritime Travel and Cultural Encounters, 1600-1900 |date=2023-12-01 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-003-81876-2 |language=en}}</ref> {{short description|Japanese feudal domain located in Hokkaidō Province}} {{Infobox former subdivision |_noautocat = |native_name = 松前藩 |conventional_long_name = Matsumae Domain |common_name = Matsumae Domain |subdivision = [[:en:Han system|Han]] |nation = |status_text = [[:en:Han system|Domain]] of Japan |government_type = Daimyō

|stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Matsumae Norihiro.jpg|right|thumb|270px|[[Matsumae Norihiro,]] the 13th next to last daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] final daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] ==History== As per the historical account in the Matsumae domain's "[[Silla Records]]," [[:en:Takeda Nobuhiro|Takeda Nobuhiro]], believed to be a descendant of the Kai-Genji and Wakasa-[[Takeda clan]]s, was the founder during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Nobuhiro succeeded Kakizaki Suishige, appointed by Masasue Ando as the shugo of Kamigoku, assumed the Kakizaki clan name, and settled in the southern part of the present-day Oshima Peninsula. Under Kakizaki Toshihiro's leadership, with the lord Ando Shunsuke, peace was established with the Ainu of Chikomotain and Hashitain, strengthening control over Ezo. Towards the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], Tamenobu Oura led an army in the Tsugaru region, resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event hastened the Kakizaki clan's independence in the Ezo region. During [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]]'s era, the son of Tokihiro, official separation from the [[:en:Andō clan|Ando clan]] (later the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) was approved by submitting to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, submission to [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] led to the recognition of the right to rule over Ezo. In the early Edo period, the lord of [[Ezo Island]] was treated as a guest vassal, but during the fifth shogun Tsunayoshi Tokugawa's time, joining the Tokuyoriai resulted in being treated as a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]. Additionally, from 1719, becoming a feudal lord with a Rank of 10,000 koku marked a significant development in the history of the Matsumae domain's governance over Ezo. At the conclusion of the Sengoku period, [[:en:Oura Tamenobu|Oura Tamenobu]] assembled a military force in the [[Tsugaru region]], resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event played a significant role in expediting the independence of the [[:en:Kakizaki clan|Kakizaki clan]] in the Ezo region. Under the leadership of [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]], the son of Tokihiro, he gained official recognition for his separation from the Ando clan (later known as the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) by pledging allegiance to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, he further solidified his position by submitting to Tokugawa Ieyasu, thereby securing the authority to govern over Ezo. Initially, during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], he was regarded as a guest vassal and held the position of the lord of Ezo Island. However, during the reign of the fifth shogun, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], he joined the Tokuyoriai and was elevated to the status of a hatamoto. Moreover, starting from 1719, he became a feudal lord with a substantial landholding of 10,000 koku.During that period, cultivating rice in Ezo was deemed unattainable, leading to the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] holding a feudal lord position without a high Rank. The designation of 10,000 koku as a Rank came later on. The black seal bestowed upon [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] by [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Ieyasu]] in 1604 granted the clan exclusive trading rights with the [[:en:Emishi people|Emishi]] ([[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]]. The feudal lord himself dispatched trading vessels to Ezo, while his retainers were entrusted with the responsibility of establishing trading posts (akinaiba) in Ezo and authorizing them to send ships for trade. The Matsumae clan implemented a policy of regulating trade between Ezo and Wajin, designating the southern [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] as [[Wajinchi]] and the remaining area as Ezochi. Ainu individuals used to journey to Wajin territories and Honshu for trade until the early Edo period, after which control became more stringent. In 1679, the Matsumae clan established an anajinya in Kusharukotan (Kusukei, Odomari-cho, Odomari-gun) in Sakhalin, initiating the development of the region as a fishing hub. Fishing was the primary industry in Wajinchi, directly managed by the Matsumae clan, but due to dwindling herring catches, individuals began migrating to Ezo for employment opportunities. By 1834, the castle town of Matsumae had evolved into a thriving urban center with a populace exceeding 10,000 residents. [[Matsumae Nobuhiro]], the 14th lord of the domain, submitted a request for the reinstatement of the domain name on June 24, 1869, and his request was approved. Subsequently, he was designated as the governor of the Matsumae domain. During that year, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] consisted of 86 districts across 11 provinces. The Matsumae Domain was established for a period of two years before it was transformed into a prefecture on July 14, 1871. Its name derived from the fact that the [[:en:Japanese imperial court|Imperial Court]] granted permission to construct a new castle in the village of Atsubu in the western region of the country. Initially, the government office was situated at Fukuyama Castle, as the construction of the new castle was interrupted by the Hakodate War. They relocated to the newly built [[Tate Castle]] and fought against the former shogunate army in the Hakodate War under the name of the Tate clan. Following the war, individuals who collaborated with the former shogunate army were apprehended and punished accordingly. [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ca:Feu de Matsumae]] [[fa:قلمرو ماتسومائه]] [[ja:松前藩]] [[ko:마쓰마에번]] [[ru:Мацумаэ (княжество)]] [[wuu:松前藩]] [[zh:松前藩]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Feu de Matsumae]][[en:Matsumae Domain]][[fa:قلمرو ماتسومائه]][[ja:松前藩]][[ko:마쓰마에번]][[ru:Мацумаэ (княжество)]][[wuu:松前藩]][[zh:松前藩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18669712]] Wikidata name: Matsumae Domain [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{refimprove|date=March 2024}} The '''Matsumae Domain''' (松前藩), a prominent domain during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], was situated in Matsumae, [[Matsumae Island]] (Ishijima), which is currently known as Matsumae Town, [[:en:Matsumae District, Hokkaido|Matsumae District]], Hokkaido, via [[:en:Tsugaru District, Mutsu|Tsugaru District]], [[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]]. The clan's leader, also known as the lord of the domain, constructed [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae]] Fukuyama Castle in the same location and henceforth, the clan came to be referred to as the Fukuyama clan. In 1868, the castle was relocated to Tatejo in Assabu-cho, Hiyama-gun, within the territory, and was known as Tatehan during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. Initially, the Matsumae clan's jurisdiction was limited to the Wajin territory on the [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] in southwest Hokkaido. However, they gradually expanded their control over the Ezo region, which is now part of Hokkaido, and transformed it into their domain. The clan's economic foundation relied on their exclusive trade with the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]] of Ezo, which set them apart from the agricultural-based governance principles of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Shogunate]] system. Towards the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shogunate often assumed control over Ezo.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Howell |first=David L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3bjADwAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PA33 |title=Capitalism From Within: Economy, Society, and the State in a Japanese Fishery |date=2018-05-04 |publisher=Univ of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-30158-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Siddle |first=Richard M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W5DKfJsPv2sC&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT47 |title=Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan |date=2012-06-14 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-82680-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Bailey |first=Scott C. M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9VzbEAAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT92 |title=Russia and Japan in the Sea of Okhotsk: A Global History of Maritime Travel and Cultural Encounters, 1600-1900 |date=2023-12-01 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-003-81876-2 |language=en}}</ref> {{short description|Japanese feudal domain located in Hokkaidō Province}} {{Infobox former subdivision |_noautocat = |native_name = 松前藩 |conventional_long_name = Matsumae Domain |common_name = Matsumae Domain |subdivision = [[:en:Han system|Han]] |nation = |status_text = [[:en:Han system|Domain]] of Japan |government_type = Daimyō
|capital = [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae Castle]] |coordinates = |political_subdiv = |today = [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]
|year_start = 1590 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post =
|image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = File:Maru ni Takedabishi (No background and Black color drawing).svg |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] |symbol = |image_map = MatsumaeJo.jpg |image_map_caption = Restored castle tower of Matsumae Castle
|stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Matsumae Norihiro.jpg|right|thumb|270px|[[Matsumae Norihiro,]] the 13th next to last daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] final daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] ==History== As per the historical account in the Matsumae domain's "[[Silla Records]]," [[:en:Takeda Nobuhiro|Takeda Nobuhiro]], believed to be a descendant of the Kai-Genji and Wakasa-[[Takeda clan]]s, was the founder during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Nobuhiro succeeded Kakizaki Suishige, appointed by Masasue Ando as the shugo of Kamigoku, assumed the Kakizaki clan name, and settled in the southern part of the present-day Oshima Peninsula. Under Kakizaki Toshihiro's leadership, with the lord Ando Shunsuke, peace was established with the Ainu of Chikomotain and Hashitain, strengthening control over Ezo. Towards the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], Tamenobu Oura led an army in the Tsugaru region, resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event hastened the Kakizaki clan's independence in the Ezo region. During [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]]'s era, the son of Tokihiro, official separation from the [[:en:Andō clan|Ando clan]] (later the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) was approved by submitting to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, submission to [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] led to the recognition of the right to rule over Ezo. In the early Edo period, the lord of [[Ezo Island]] was treated as a guest vassal, but during the fifth shogun Tsunayoshi Tokugawa's time, joining the Tokuyoriai resulted in being treated as a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]. Additionally, from 1719, becoming a feudal lord with a Rank of 10,000 koku marked a significant development in the history of the Matsumae domain's governance over Ezo. At the conclusion of the Sengoku period, [[:en:Oura Tamenobu|Oura Tamenobu]] assembled a military force in the [[Tsugaru region]], resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event played a significant role in expediting the independence of the [[:en:Kakizaki clan|Kakizaki clan]] in the Ezo region. Under the leadership of [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]], the son of Tokihiro, he gained official recognition for his separation from the Ando clan (later known as the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) by pledging allegiance to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, he further solidified his position by submitting to Tokugawa Ieyasu, thereby securing the authority to govern over Ezo. Initially, during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], he was regarded as a guest vassal and held the position of the lord of Ezo Island. However, during the reign of the fifth shogun, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], he joined the Tokuyoriai and was elevated to the status of a hatamoto. Moreover, starting from 1719, he became a feudal lord with a substantial landholding of 10,000 koku.During that period, cultivating rice in Ezo was deemed unattainable, leading to the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] holding a feudal lord position without a high Rank. The designation of 10,000 koku as a Rank came later on. The black seal bestowed upon [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] by [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Ieyasu]] in 1604 granted the clan exclusive trading rights with the [[:en:Emishi people|Emishi]] ([[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]]. Trade commodities like salmon, kelp, herring, and furs brought in revenue, reportedly amounting to 70,000 koku. The feudal lord himself dispatched trading vessels to Ezo, while his retainers were entrusted with the responsibility of establishing trading posts (akinaiba) in Ezo and authorizing them to send ships for trade. The Matsumae clan implemented a policy of regulating trade between Ezo and Wajin, designating the southern [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] as [[Wajinchi]] and the remaining area as Ezochi. Ainu individuals used to journey to Wajin territories and Honshu for trade until the early Edo period, after which control became more stringent. In 1679, the Matsumae clan established an anajinya in Kusharukotan (Kusukei, Odomari-cho, Odomari-gun) in Sakhalin, initiating the development of the region as a fishing hub. Fishing was the primary industry in Wajinchi, directly managed by the Matsumae clan, but due to dwindling herring catches, individuals began migrating to Ezo for employment opportunities. By 1834, the castle town of Matsumae had evolved into a thriving urban center with a populace exceeding 10,000 residents. [[Matsumae Nobuhiro]], the 14th lord of the domain, submitted a request for the reinstatement of the domain name on June 24, 1869, and his request was approved. Subsequently, he was designated as the governor of the Matsumae domain. During that year, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] consisted of 86 districts across 11 provinces. The Matsumae Domain was established for a period of two years before it was transformed into a prefecture on July 14, 1871. Its name derived from the fact that the [[:en:Japanese imperial court|Imperial Court]] granted permission to construct a new castle in the village of Atsubu in the western region of the country. Initially, the government office was situated at Fukuyama Castle, as the construction of the new castle was interrupted by the Hakodate War. During the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] and the [[Tohoku War]], the Matsumae clan joined forces with the Ou-etsu clan alliance, but the loyalist Seigi-tai took control of the domain and defected to the new government. They relocated to the newly built [[Tate Castle]] and fought against the former shogunate army in the Hakodate War under the name of the Tate clan. Following the war, individuals who collaborated with the former shogunate army were apprehended and punished accordingly. Over 90 individuals, including both townspeople and samurai, faced punishment, with 19 of them being publicly executed. ==Holdings at the end of the Edo period== **[[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]] *[[Erzhi County]] *[[Hiyama-gun]] *[[Tsugaru-gun]] *[[Fukushima-gun]] **[[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] *[[Ryuhi Cape]] *[[:en:Iwashiro Province|Iwashiro Province]] *[[Date County]] - |stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Matsumae Norihiro.jpg|right|thumb|270px|[[Matsumae Norihiro,]] the 13th next to last daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] final daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] ==History== As per the historical account in the Matsumae domain's "[[Silla Records]]," [[:en:Takeda Nobuhiro|Takeda Nobuhiro]], believed to be a descendant of the Kai-Genji and Wakasa-[[Takeda clan]]s, was the founder during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Nobuhiro succeeded Kakizaki Suishige, appointed by Masasue Ando as the shugo of Kamigoku, assumed the Kakizaki clan name, and settled in the southern part of the present-day Oshima Peninsula. Under Kakizaki Toshihiro's leadership, with the lord Ando Shunsuke, peace was established with the Ainu of Chikomotain and Hashitain, strengthening control over Ezo. Towards the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], Tamenobu Oura led an army in the Tsugaru region, resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event hastened the Kakizaki clan's independence in the Ezo region. During [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]]'s era, the son of Tokihiro, official separation from the [[:en:Andō clan|Ando clan]] (later the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) was approved by submitting to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, submission to [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] led to the recognition of the right to rule over Ezo. In the early Edo period, the lord of [[Ezo Island]] was treated as a guest vassal, but during the fifth shogun Tsunayoshi Tokugawa's time, joining the Tokuyoriai resulted in being treated as a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]. Additionally, from 1719, becoming a feudal lord with a Rank of 10,000 koku marked a significant development in the history of the Matsumae domain's governance over Ezo. At the conclusion of the Sengoku period, [[:en:Oura Tamenobu|Oura Tamenobu]] assembled a military force in the [[Tsugaru region]], resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event played a significant role in expediting the independence of the [[:en:Kakizaki clan|Kakizaki clan]] in the Ezo region. Under the leadership of [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]], the son of Tokihiro, he gained official recognition for his separation from the Ando clan (later known as the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) by pledging allegiance to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, he further solidified his position by submitting to Tokugawa Ieyasu, thereby securing the authority to govern over Ezo. Initially, during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], he was regarded as a guest vassal and held the position of the lord of Ezo Island. However, during the reign of the fifth shogun, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], he joined the Tokuyoriai and was elevated to the status of a hatamoto. Moreover, starting from 1719, he became a feudal lord with a substantial landholding of 10,000 koku.During that period, cultivating rice in Ezo was deemed unattainable, leading to the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] holding a feudal lord position without a high Rank. The designation of 10,000 koku as a Rank came later on. The black seal bestowed upon [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] by [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Ieyasu]] in 1604 granted the clan exclusive trading rights with the [[:en:Emishi people|Emishi]] ([[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]]. Trade commodities like salmon, kelp, herring, and furs brought in revenue, reportedly amounting to 70,000 koku. The feudal lord himself dispatched trading vessels to Ezo, while his retainers were entrusted with the responsibility of establishing trading posts (akinaiba) in Ezo and authorizing them to send ships for trade. The Matsumae clan implemented a policy of regulating trade between Ezo and Wajin, designating the southern [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] as [[Wajinchi]] and the remaining area as Ezochi. Ainu individuals used to journey to Wajin territories and Honshu for trade until the early Edo period, after which control became more stringent. In 1679, the Matsumae clan established an anajinya in Kusharukotan (Kusukei, Odomari-cho, Odomari-gun) in Sakhalin, initiating the development of the region as a fishing hub. Fishing was the primary industry in Wajinchi, directly managed by the Matsumae clan, but due to dwindling herring catches, individuals began migrating to Ezo for employment opportunities. By 1834, the castle town of Matsumae had evolved into a thriving urban center with a populace exceeding 10,000 residents. [[Matsumae Nobuhiro]], the 14th lord of the domain, submitted a request for the reinstatement of the domain name on June 24, 1869, and his request was approved. Subsequently, he was designated as the governor of the Matsumae domain. During that year, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] consisted of 86 districts across 11 provinces. The Matsumae Domain was established for a period of two years before it was transformed into a prefecture on July 14, 1871. Its name derived from the fact that the [[:en:Japanese imperial court|Imperial Court]] granted permission to construct a new castle in the village of Atsubu in the western region of the country. Initially, the government office was situated at Fukuyama Castle, as the construction of the new castle was interrupted by the Hakodate War. During the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] and the [[Tohoku War]], the Matsumae clan joined forces with the Ou-etsu clan alliance, but the loyalist Seigi-tai took control of the domain and defected to the new government. They relocated to the newly built [[Tate Castle]] and fought against the former shogunate army in the Hakodate War under the name of the Tate clan. Following the war, individuals who collaborated with the former shogunate army were apprehended and punished accordingly. Over 90 individuals, including both townspeople and samurai, faced punishment, with 19 of them being publicly executed. ==Holdings at the end of the Edo period== **[[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]] *[[Erzhi County]] *[[Hiyama-gun]] *[[Tsugaru-gun]] *[[Fukushima-gun]] **[[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] *[[Ryuhi Cape]] *[[:en:Iwashiro Province|Iwashiro Province]] *[[Date County]] - <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Feu de Matsumae]][[en:Matsumae Domain]][[fa:قلمرو ماتسومائه]][[ja:松前藩]][[ko:마쓰마에번]][[ru:Мацумаэ (княжество)]][[wuu:松前藩]][[zh:松前藩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18669712]] Wikidata name: Matsumae Domain [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{refimprove|date=March 2024}} The '''Matsumae Domain''' (松前藩), a prominent domain during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], was situated in Matsumae, [[Matsumae Island]] (Ishijima), which is currently known as Matsumae Town, [[:en:Matsumae District, Hokkaido|Matsumae District]], Hokkaido, via [[:en:Tsugaru District, Mutsu|Tsugaru District]], [[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]]. The clan's leader, also known as the lord of the domain, constructed [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae]] Fukuyama Castle in the same location and henceforth, the clan came to be referred to as the Fukuyama clan. In 1868, the castle was relocated to Tatejo in Assabu-cho, Hiyama-gun, within the territory, and was known as Tatehan during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. Initially, the Matsumae clan's jurisdiction was limited to the Wajin territory on the [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] in southwest Hokkaido. However, they gradually expanded their control over the Ezo region, which is now part of Hokkaido, and transformed it into their domain. The clan's economic foundation relied on their exclusive trade with the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]] of Ezo, which set them apart from the agricultural-based governance principles of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Shogunate]] system. Towards the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shogunate often assumed control over Ezo.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Howell |first=David L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3bjADwAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PA33 |title=Capitalism From Within: Economy, Society, and the State in a Japanese Fishery |date=2018-05-04 |publisher=Univ of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-30158-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Siddle |first=Richard M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W5DKfJsPv2sC&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT47 |title=Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan |date=2012-06-14 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-82680-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Bailey |first=Scott C. M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9VzbEAAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT92 |title=Russia and Japan in the Sea of Okhotsk: A Global History of Maritime Travel and Cultural Encounters, 1600-1900 |date=2023-12-01 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-003-81876-2 |language=en}}</ref> {{short description|Japanese feudal domain located in Hokkaidō Province}} {{Infobox former subdivision |_noautocat = |native_name = 松前藩 |conventional_long_name = Matsumae Domain |common_name = Matsumae Domain |subdivision = [[:en:Han system|Han]] |nation = |status_text = [[:en:Han system|Domain]] of Japan |government_type = Daimyō

{{wikidata link|Q18669712}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Feu de Matsumae]][[en:Matsumae Domain]][[fa:قلمرو ماتسومائه]][[ja:松前藩]][[ko:마쓰마에번]][[ru:Мацумаэ (княжество)]][[wuu:松前藩]][[zh:松前藩]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q18669712]] Wikidata name: Matsumae Domain [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{refimprove|date=March 2024}} The '''Matsumae Domain''' (松前藩), a prominent domain during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], was situated in Matsumae, [[Matsumae Island]] (Ishijima), which is currently known as Matsumae Town, [[:en:Matsumae District, Hokkaido|Matsumae District]], Hokkaido, via [[:en:Tsugaru District, Mutsu|Tsugaru District]], [[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]]. The clan's leader, also known as the lord of the domain, constructed [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae]] Fukuyama Castle in the same location and henceforth, the clan came to be referred to as the Fukuyama clan. In 1868, the castle was relocated to Tatejo in Assabu-cho, Hiyama-gun, within the territory, and was known as Tatehan during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]]. Initially, the Matsumae clan's jurisdiction was limited to the Wajin territory on the [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] in southwest Hokkaido. However, they gradually expanded their control over the Ezo region, which is now part of Hokkaido, and transformed it into their domain. The clan's economic foundation relied on their exclusive trade with the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]] of Ezo, which set them apart from the agricultural-based governance principles of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Shogunate]] system. Towards the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shogunate often assumed control over Ezo.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Howell |first=David L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3bjADwAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PA33 |title=Capitalism From Within: Economy, Society, and the State in a Japanese Fishery |date=2018-05-04 |publisher=Univ of California Press |isbn=978-0-520-30158-0 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Siddle |first=Richard M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W5DKfJsPv2sC&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT47 |title=Race, Resistance and the Ainu of Japan |date=2012-06-14 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-82680-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Bailey |first=Scott C. M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9VzbEAAAQBAJ&dq=Matsumae+Domain&pg=PT92 |title=Russia and Japan in the Sea of Okhotsk: A Global History of Maritime Travel and Cultural Encounters, 1600-1900 |date=2023-12-01 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-003-81876-2 |language=en}}</ref> {{short description|Japanese feudal domain located in Hokkaidō Province}} {{Infobox former subdivision |_noautocat = |native_name = 松前藩 |conventional_long_name = Matsumae Domain |common_name = Matsumae Domain |subdivision = [[:en:Han system|Han]] |nation = |status_text = [[:en:Han system|Domain]] of Japan |government_type = Daimyō
|capital = [[:en:Matsumae Castle|Matsumae Castle]] |coordinates = |political_subdiv = |today = [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]
|year_start = 1590 |year_end = 1871 |event_start = |date_start = |event_end = |date_end = |event1 = |date_event1 = |event2 = |date_event2 = |event3 = |date_event3 = |event4 = |date_event4 = |event5 = |date_event5 = |life_span = |era = Edo period |event_pre = |date_pre = |event_post = |date_post =
|image_flag = |image_border = |flag_type = |flag = |image_coat = File:Maru ni Takedabishi (No background and Black color drawing).svg |symbol_type = ''[[Mon (emblem)|Mon]]'' of the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] |symbol = |image_map = MatsumaeJo.jpg |image_map_caption = Restored castle tower of Matsumae Castle
|stat_year1 = |stat_area1 = |stat_pop1 = |footnotes = }} [[File:Matsumae Norihiro.jpg|right|thumb|270px|[[Matsumae Norihiro,]] the 13th next to last daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] final daimyo of Matsumae Domain]] ==History== As per the historical account in the Matsumae domain's "[[Silla Records]]," [[:en:Takeda Nobuhiro|Takeda Nobuhiro]], believed to be a descendant of the Kai-Genji and Wakasa-[[Takeda clan]]s, was the founder during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. Nobuhiro succeeded Kakizaki Suishige, appointed by Masasue Ando as the shugo of Kamigoku, assumed the Kakizaki clan name, and settled in the southern part of the present-day Oshima Peninsula. Under Kakizaki Toshihiro's leadership, with the lord Ando Shunsuke, peace was established with the Ainu of Chikomotain and Hashitain, strengthening control over Ezo. Towards the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], Tamenobu Oura led an army in the Tsugaru region, resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event hastened the Kakizaki clan's independence in the Ezo region. During [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]]'s era, the son of Tokihiro, official separation from the [[:en:Andō clan|Ando clan]] (later the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) was approved by submitting to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, submission to [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] led to the recognition of the right to rule over Ezo. In the early Edo period, the lord of [[Ezo Island]] was treated as a guest vassal, but during the fifth shogun Tsunayoshi Tokugawa's time, joining the Tokuyoriai resulted in being treated as a [[:en:Hatamoto|Hatamoto]]. Additionally, from 1719, becoming a feudal lord with a Rank of 10,000 koku marked a significant development in the history of the Matsumae domain's governance over Ezo. At the conclusion of the Sengoku period, [[:en:Oura Tamenobu|Oura Tamenobu]] assembled a military force in the [[Tsugaru region]], resulting in the expulsion of the Ezo Kanrei (Hiyama Ando clan) from Tsugaru. This event played a significant role in expediting the independence of the [[:en:Kakizaki clan|Kakizaki clan]] in the Ezo region. Under the leadership of [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]], the son of Tokihiro, he gained official recognition for his separation from the Ando clan (later known as the [[:en:Akita clan|Akita clan]]) by pledging allegiance to Toyotomi Hideyoshi. In 1599, he further solidified his position by submitting to Tokugawa Ieyasu, thereby securing the authority to govern over Ezo. Initially, during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], he was regarded as a guest vassal and held the position of the lord of Ezo Island. However, during the reign of the fifth shogun, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], he joined the Tokuyoriai and was elevated to the status of a hatamoto. Moreover, starting from 1719, he became a feudal lord with a substantial landholding of 10,000 koku.During that period, cultivating rice in Ezo was deemed unattainable, leading to the [[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]] holding a feudal lord position without a high Rank. The designation of 10,000 koku as a Rank came later on. The black seal bestowed upon [[:en:Matsumae Yoshihiro|Matsumae Yoshihiro]] by [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Ieyasu]] in 1604 granted the clan exclusive trading rights with the [[:en:Emishi people|Emishi]] ([[:en:Ainu people|Ainu people]]. Trade commodities like salmon, kelp, herring, and furs brought in revenue, reportedly amounting to 70,000 koku. The feudal lord himself dispatched trading vessels to Ezo, while his retainers were entrusted with the responsibility of establishing trading posts (akinaiba) in Ezo and authorizing them to send ships for trade. The Matsumae clan implemented a policy of regulating trade between Ezo and Wajin, designating the southern [[:en:Oshima Peninsula|Oshima Peninsula]] as [[Wajinchi]] and the remaining area as Ezochi. Ainu individuals used to journey to Wajin territories and Honshu for trade until the early Edo period, after which control became more stringent. In 1679, the Matsumae clan established an anajinya in Kusharukotan (Kusukei, Odomari-cho, Odomari-gun) in Sakhalin, initiating the development of the region as a fishing hub. Fishing was the primary industry in Wajinchi, directly managed by the Matsumae clan, but due to dwindling herring catches, individuals began migrating to Ezo for employment opportunities. By 1834, the castle town of Matsumae had evolved into a thriving urban center with a populace exceeding 10,000 residents. [[Matsumae Nobuhiro]], the 14th lord of the domain, submitted a request for the reinstatement of the domain name on June 24, 1869, and his request was approved. Subsequently, he was designated as the governor of the Matsumae domain. During that year, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] consisted of 86 districts across 11 provinces. The Matsumae Domain was established for a period of two years before it was transformed into a prefecture on July 14, 1871. Its name derived from the fact that the [[:en:Japanese imperial court|Imperial Court]] granted permission to construct a new castle in the village of Atsubu in the western region of the country. Initially, the government office was situated at Fukuyama Castle, as the construction of the new castle was interrupted by the Hakodate War. During the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] and the [[Tohoku War]], the Matsumae clan joined forces with the Ou-etsu clan alliance, but the loyalist Seigi-tai took control of the domain and defected to the new government. They relocated to the newly built [[Tate Castle]] and fought against the former shogunate army in the Hakodate War under the name of the Tate clan. Following the war, individuals who collaborated with the former shogunate army were apprehended and punished accordingly. Over 90 individuals, including both townspeople and samurai, faced punishment, with 19 of them being publicly executed. ==Holdings at the end of the Edo period== **[[:en:Oshima Province|Oshima Province]] *[[Erzhi County]] *[[Hiyama-gun]] *[[Tsugaru-gun]] *[[Fukushima-gun]] **[[:en:Mutsu Province|Mutsu Province]] *[[Ryuhi Cape]] *[[:en:Iwashiro Province|Iwashiro Province]] *[[Date County]] - 10 villages *[[Murayama County]] - 39 villages == List of daimyo == :{| class=wikitable ! #||Name || Tenure || Courtesy title || Court Rank || ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' |- |colspan=6| [[File:Maru ni Takedabishi (No background and Black color drawing).svg |25px]] '''[[:en:Matsumae clan|Matsumae clan]],''' 1590 -
